item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that's used in this article is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image comes from here under Public Domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We should move -1 to the moon, then we can figure out if there's subsurface water :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That incident report was wonderful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! That means a lot coming from you. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome. Just awesome. +3.14

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fucking incredible. I really really like how this plays out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whoops! Fix'd.

And yeah, I'm really pleased with how the Incident Report turned out. Really ties the whole thing together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was ambivalent until I got to the incident report. Now I'm glad, because I can upvote you twice.

One minor nitpick, though: that oil tanker that went down on February 30th, 1943.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I'll never tell~

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe he knows when -2 is coming and reacted accordingly?

…I'd say that was the author's intent.

You fool, Author is DEAD!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think "you forgot about the water table, you fucking idiot" confirms that -1 is hostile. The original, longer version of the interview in the draft had a section where the interviewer pulls out some historical records and demolishes -1's story, so I'd say that was the author's intent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The way I figure, there's two ways to interpret this. Either -1 is a victim, but he's a repeat victim, someone who will never escape this thing, or -1 is just as much in on it as -2, possibly being an avatar of the whole. The last line of the interview doesn't really clue completely towards either interpretation. In the first version, it's very possible that -1 is just one in a long line of baits used by the monster. In the second, -1 may just be wearing whatever seems fashionable in order to get "rescued." Either one is horrifying, but in its own unique way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanon: The "large marine creature" isn't a pre-1900s distorted report of a submarine, 2770 has "updated" -1 and -2 several times over the centuries, and before the submarine was invented there actually was a giant whale that popped out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless you meant the quotee to be mis-speaking, it's "arctic", not "artic".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much, yeah. However, it the "lure" has to be rescued, otherwise it has no idea where to "pull". It took a little longer with the Incident since the lure was in the middle of a desert far, far away from the North Sea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's like an anglerfish or maybe I'm thinking an archerfish. Something that lures its prey into the water before swallowing it whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really pretty good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought it was pretty good until I read the supplement. It's fantastic. +1

Before I read the supplement, I thought it might be easily possible to construct an unmanned rescue craft capable of surviving being pulled underwater at 200 km/h. The submarine might be unstoppable in manifesting (perhaps unless you launched SCP-2770-1 into space), but would it also destroy any kind of tracking or recording devices?

What if 2770-1 was rescued by another submarine, a very well-built, durable sub, not unlike this one: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deepsea_Challenger with a bunch of cameras and GPS, manned by a single D-class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to down vote until the supplement page. That was fantastic. Up vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The SCP itself is relatively bland and failed to really attract my interest, but the interview log in the supplement got me.

"I think you forgot about the water table, you fucking idiot."

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I get from this and the interview log is that this thing is like a monk fish, it attracts its pray with a lure (the shipwrecked sailor) than goes in for the kill once the pray has taken the bait. While this has been done before, this instance seems to have enough uniqueness to stand out on its own. The interview log however is a bit unwieldily, I would recommend shortening it, removing some of the drama, and making the reveal a bit less blatant and more interesting (The lure isn't charismatic in its trickery, it does not need to be as in is merely a worm on a line. maybe make the lure more of a insentient, poorly painted guise; constantly thanking its "savers", and inconsistent in its story.). Most importantly the two pages should be melded.

+1 for the SCP

No vote for the interview log

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looking at it now, I think it's better to just only tell about that one old ship and leave the reader to assume it's probably nabbed a shitload more in the time between the newspaper report and when they actually realize it existed. Otherwise it feels redundant and in either case having two activity logs just looked weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. My only nitpick structurally might be that I'd put confirmed activity before suspected but that's a minor thing and arguments could be made both ways. The blackboxing is maybe a bit more than needed, I guess; in particular, if any/many of the activity dates correspond to real-world events it seems unnecessary. Tone is good overall. I'm tentatively upvoting with the only caveat that, between this and the supplement, it feels like a simple lure (albeit very strong one) and I'm unsure what makes this stand out from other such skips. I'll reread in a couple days.
